title: Maximum Heart Rate Calculator (by Age)
shortDescription: Estimate your maximum heart rate from your age in beats per minute (bpm).
description: This maximum heart rate calculator estimates your peak heart rate based on your age. It uses the classic Fox formula (MHR = 220 − age) and also shows the more recent Tanaka estimate (208 − 0.7 × age), giving results in beats per minute (bpm). It also outlines common training heart-rate zones to guide your workouts.
metaDescription: Free maximum heart rate calculator. Estimate your peak heart rate (bpm) from your age using the Fox and Tanaka formulas, plus training zones.

result.label: Maximum heart rate
result.empty: Enter your age to estimate your maximum heart rate.

sections.heading: What is maximum heart rate?
paragraphs: Your maximum heart rate (MHR) is the highest number of times your heart can beat per minute during intense exertion. It is most often estimated from age because measuring it directly requires a supervised stress test.
The classic Fox formula is MHR = 220 − age. This tool also shows the Tanaka estimate (208 − 0.7 × age), which research suggests is more accurate across a wide range of ages.

sections.heading: Using heart rate zones for training
paragraphs: Training zones are percentages of your maximum heart rate. Easy aerobic work sits around 60–70%, tempo efforts around 70–80%, and high-intensity intervals above 80%.
Knowing your MHR helps you target the right intensity for fat burning, endurance, or peak performance without overtraining.

howTo.name: Enter your age
howTo.text: Type your current age in years into the input field.

howTo.name: Read your estimate
howTo.text: The calculator instantly shows your maximum heart rate in beats per minute (bpm).

howTo.name: Apply training zones
howTo.text: Multiply your MHR by zone percentages to set target heart rates for your workouts.

faq.q: How accurate is the 220 − age formula?
faq.a: It is a rough population average. Individual maximum heart rate can vary by 10–20 bpm, so treat it as a general estimate.

faq.q: Which formula should I trust, Fox or Tanaka?
faq.a: The Tanaka estimate (208 − 0.7 × age) tends to be more accurate for older adults, while the Fox formula is simpler and widely used.

faq.q: What is a heart rate zone?
faq.a: A zone is a range expressed as a percentage of your maximum heart rate, used to target specific training effects like endurance or speed.

faq.q: Can I measure my true maximum heart rate?
faq.a: Yes, through a supervised maximal exercise test, but this should only be done with medical guidance, especially if you have health conditions.
No. This calculator provides a general estimate only and is not medical advice. Consult a healthcare professional before starting intense exercise.
